items.definition: to make a bad or unsatisfactory situation better.
items.example: The new ventilation system did much to ameliorate the stuffy conditions in the old library.

context_question.stem: The new ventilation system did much to ______ the stuffy conditions in the old library.
context_question.explanation: To ameliorate is to make something bad better. 'Exacerbate' is the opposite; 'fabricate' and 'postpone' do not fit.

items.definition: stated or appearing to be true, but not necessarily so.
items.example: The ostensible reason for the meeting was to celebrate, but everyone suspected layoffs were coming.

context_question.stem: The ______ reason for the meeting was to celebrate, but everyone suspected layoffs were coming.
context_question.explanation: 'Ostensible' marks a stated reason that may differ from the real one, which the contrast with 'but everyone suspected' signals.

items.example: She summarized the report's salient points in under two minutes.

context_question.stem: She summarized the report's ______ points in under two minutes.
context_question.explanation: Salient points are the most important ones, which is what a brief summary would cover.

items.definition: very weak or slight; lacking a sound basis.
items.example: The detective's theory rested on tenuous evidence that collapsed under questioning.

context_question.stem: The detective's theory rested on ______ evidence that collapsed under questioning.
context_question.explanation: Evidence that 'collapsed under questioning' was weak, i.e., tenuous. The other options imply strength or quantity.

items.example: Smartphones have become so ubiquitous that it is rare to see anyone without one.

context_question.stem: Smartphones have become so ______ that it is rare to see anyone without one.
context_question.explanation: If it is rare to see anyone without one, the item is everywhere, i.e., ubiquitous.

items.definition: the quality of being open and honest; frankness.
items.example: The witness answered with surprising candor, admitting facts that hurt her own case.

context_question.stem: The witness answered with surprising ______, admitting facts that hurt her own case.
context_question.explanation: Admitting facts that hurt one's own case is a mark of candor (frank honesty).

items.definition: acting with or showing care and thought for the future; sensible.
items.example: It is prudent to keep several months of savings in case of an emergency.

context_question.stem: It is ______ to keep several months of savings in case of an emergency.
context_question.explanation: Preparing sensibly for the future is the definition of being prudent; 'reckless' is its opposite.

items.definition: (of an argument or case) clear, logical, and convincing.
items.example: Her cogent argument persuaded even the skeptics on the committee.

context_question.stem: Her ______ argument persuaded even the skeptics on the committee.
context_question.explanation: An argument that persuades skeptics is clear and convincing, i.e., cogent. 'Incoherent' is the opposite; length and hesitation do not imply persuasiveness.

items.definition: open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or deliberately unclear.
items.example: The spokesperson gave an equivocal answer that neither confirmed nor denied the rumor.

context_question.stem: The spokesperson gave an ______ answer that neither confirmed nor denied the rumor.
context_question.explanation: An answer that neither confirms nor denies is ambiguous, i.e., equivocal. 'Definitive' is the opposite.

items.definition: to weaken or damage something, especially gradually or insidiously.
items.example: Constant criticism can undermine a young athlete's confidence.

context_question.stem: Constant criticism can ______ a young athlete's confidence.
context_question.explanation: To undermine confidence is to weaken it gradually, which is what constant criticism does; 'reinforce' is the opposite.
